I've just added a note about the use of *deque*. To expand on that: this is really an implementation detail. All I wanted is to be able to identify a typed list in the next phase of encoding. (Because we serialise containers into a list already, I can't use a list again to represent the typed list).

So I was looking for a _different_ type that hasn't been used. One is to use a set which should be more natural (because, e.g. a leaf-list should behave like a set -- unique items) but the set implementation doesn't maintain ordering and I didn't want to write an `OrderedSet` just for this. A `deque`  behaves like a list and maintains ordering so I used it.

Perhaps, we could actually flip this around => make a container a set (since ordering doesn't need to be maintained AFAIK?) and then => typed list as list ?

Sure fair concern. So if it gets stuck on the `wait` for some reason the CPU won't spin, it just won't log. Spinning the CPU would happen if the thread was not shutting down but for some reason `len(self.batch.entries)` wasn't being reset to 0 after the commit ,or if `len` returned <0 which I think is impossible. Otherwise it will just get stuck in the wait until a notify which is only called atexit or when a log message is sent.

`del self.entries[:]` is called at the end of `Batch.commit`, so I guess one fear might be is if for some reason that wasn't called. Maybe we should put the del in a try: finally to prevent that but I think the exception would bubble up and kill the entire worker thread anyway. If the network calls just hang then the thread will just hang.

Okay, so this one is going to need to be more sophisticated. Here's what I think is the rough logic.

* Look in the sectPr for a headerReference of the right description (type="default" I think in this case).
* If not found, return None
* If it is found, get the rId from it, that's our key to opening the part.
* call self.part.related_header_body(rId).

  This method doesn't exist yet, so it will need to be mocked in the test. A grep on 'method_mock' in the tests will find examples.

  This method basically delegates getting the right header.xml part and loading it into a HeaderBody object, to the document part (DocumentPart) object. All relationship traversal and general part finagling is the responsibility of another part (happens at the part <-> part level). docx.parts.document.DocumentPart.numbering_part is some sort of example; there are a couple other examples on that class as well.

* HeaderBody will inherit from BlockItemContainer. BlockItemContainer is only a base class, not used directly. There will at some time be a HeaderPart class as well, in docx.parts.header.py, not sure when that will come in but it will be distinct from Header and HeaderBody.
* return header_body (the return value of self.part.related_header_body(rId)).

Just as a sketch I think it might look roughly like this:

```python
headerReference = self._sectPr.headerReference_of_type(self._type)
if headerReference is None:
    return None
return self.part.related_header_body(headerReference.rId)
```

The analysis document should also be elaborated a bit to show an XML specimen of an actual header (w:hdr at root), say with a single paragraph containing 'Foobar' or whatever. The rich details are all defined by BlockItemContainer so don't need to be repeated there. Also we'll want the XML Schema bits that rule w:hdr and any other bits and pieces you notice we're likely to need. And the parts of the protocol that we're into now should be added as well if they're not there yet. It might make sense to actually assemble a second document, the first for header access (getting ahold of a header) and the second for header properties (working with one once you actually have it in hand :).

I expect by now your starting to see that the analysis document is not the idle exercise it perhaps initially appeared to be, but rather is a collection of all the things we need now to complete the implementation :) In fact, I didn't do those initially, it was only the suffering (in the form of rework) for the lack of that information that drove me to that extra first step :)

Don't do this, there are two methods for a reason, if the caller wants to control the wait timeout it must use the `token_swap_async` and call `wait(timeout)`.

Think about it, adding a timeout as an argument through multiple layers is a recipe for bad code, every new function needs to set a default value, which may turn out different because of no good reason, and every layer must pass downwards it's notion of timeout, being quite easy to forget while refactoring.

returning an async result allows the caller to do whatever it pleases, it may wait forever or use a timeout, there is no loss of generality in the code, and we don't need to worry about the above.

We considered three options: `NodeProto`, `GraphProto`, and `ModelProto`. However, when we tried to map existing vendor libraries to the backend API with the first two options, we realized that they lack important context details:

- `NodeProto` doesn't internally contain information about input and output shapes, and this information is important to decide is an operator is supported. Many of today's vendor libraries have a limit on maximum shapes that can handle, and we know one vendor library that imposes divisibility restrictions on number of input and output channels (i.e. number of channels must be a multiple of 8, otherwise operator is unsupported), thus input/output shapes are crucial for `onnxGetBackendCompatibility` operation.

- `GraphProto` is a better candidate as it provides input/output shapes and data types, but `GraphProto` still lacks crucial information to validate the graph: `opset_import` and `ir_version`.

Thus, we settled on `ModelProto` for the proposal. It has the additional advantage that much code implementing and using `onnxInitGraph` and `onnxGetBackendCompatibility` can be shared.

It allows mypy to find typing information (`.pyi` files or `.py` files) in the `stubs/` directory and in the `third_party/pybind11` directory (`pybind11` for the corresponding module and `stubs` for some general local overrides)
It would be far better to model this as dynamic scoping (which now has the correct properties), but you can always desugar dynamic scoping into explicit parameter passing and that keeps ONNX simpler.
